title: Slag Disposal
desc: Forge waste goes in the lava. The lava doesn't always want it.

steps.text:
Every forge produces slag — the waste metal, impurities, and failed castings that can't be reused. In the UnderWorld, slag disposal is simple: dump it in an approved lava flow and let the magma consume it.

Simple in theory. In practice, you're pushing a cart full of hot metal scraps down a narrow tunnel toward a river of molten rock, hoping the cart doesn't tip.

The disposal worker is ahead of you, checking the flow with a long iron pole. "Current's good today. Flowing east. That means the slag goes in on the west bank and gets carried away. If the current reverses, we wait. Reverse-flow slag piles up and forms dams."

She jabs the pole into the lava. It comes out glowing orange. "West bank. Go."

options.label: Tip the cart from the bank
Toss the slag in piece by piece for control

steps.text:
You angle the cart to the bank's edge and tip. The slag slides out in a clattering cascade of blackened metal, pouring into the lava flow in a hissing, sparking avalanche.

The lava accepts most of it. Pieces sink immediately, consumed. But three larger chunks — failed castings, warped and heavy — sit on the surface, glowing, refusing to melt.

"Dense alloy," the worker says, frowning. "Lava temp's not high enough. We'll have to pole them into the hot channel." She hands you the iron pole.

You push. The chunks resist, grinding against the current. One finally catches the main flow and is dragged under. The second follows. The third sticks on a shelf of cooled lava and won't budge.

"Leave it. It'll melt eventually." She shrugs. "Or it'll become part of the landscape. Either way, the cart's empty. Job done."

options.label: Push the empty cart back up the tunnel

steps.text:
You pick up pieces and lob them in one at a time. Each one hisses on contact — small pieces dissolving instantly, larger ones floating for a moment before sinking. It's strangely satisfying. Like feeding a very hot, very patient animal.

The worker watches, amused. "Therapeutic, isn't it? Some of the disposal crew say this is the best job in the UnderWorld. No politics. No fighting. Just you and the lava."

You throw a warped blade. It spins through the air and hits the lava flat, sending up a spray of orange sparks. The worker whistles appreciatively.

The cart empties piece by piece. The lava consumes everything without complaint. When you're done, the flow looks exactly the same as before — unmarked, unhurried, indifferent to the fact that it just ate thirty pounds of failed metalwork.

"Same time tomorrow," the worker says. "The forges never stop, so neither do we."
